title: Full-Stack Flow — NxGN Actions
purpose: **AI Summary**: End-to-end data flow narratives showing how user actions travel through the Angular frontend and .NET API backend. Traces five core operations (create, edit, grid load, delete, aggregate with children) for the Actions domain, explaining the contract boundary between web and API at each step.

examples.name: The Contract Boundary Example 1
examples.code:
┌─────────────────────────────────┐         ┌─────────────────────────────────┐
│           Angular Web           │         │           .NET API              │
│                                 │         │                                 │
│  interface ActionDTO {          │  ←──→   │  class ActionDTO {              │
│    id: string;                  │  HTTP   │    Guid Id { get; set; }        │
│    name: string;                │  JSON   │    string Name { get; set; }    │
│    priority: EnumDTO;           │         │    EnumDTO Priority ...         │
│    status: EnumDTO;             │         │    EnumDTO Status ...           │
│    evidence: EvidenceDTO[];     │         │    List<EvidenceDTO> Evidence   │
│  }                              │         │  }                              │
│                                 │         │                                 │
│  // GET → DTO → mutate → POST  │         │  // SaveRequest inherits DTO    │
│  // Same shape in and out       │         │  // Guid.Empty = create         │
└─────────────────────────────────┘         └─────────────────────────────────┘

examples.name: Flow 1: Create an Action Example 1
examples.code:
ActionListComponent
  │ onAdd()
  │ router.navigate(['/actions/new'])
  ▼
Angular Router
  │ Matches: { path: 'actions/new', loadComponent: ActionCaptureComponent }
  │ No :id param → id input = undefined
  ▼
ActionCaptureComponent
  │ isEditMode = computed(() => GUID_REGEX.test(undefined)) → false
  │ Name: "New Action"
  │ Form signals: name='', description='', etc.

examples.name: Flow 1: Create an Action Example 2
examples.code:
User selects an active catalogue lookup value:
selectedActionType.set({ code: "CORRECTIVE", name: "Corrective" })
selectedStatus.set({ id: 1, name: "Open" })

examples.name: Flow 1: Create an Action Example 3
examples.code:
Save request:
{
  id: "00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000",
  name: "Perform Safety Inspection",
  actionTypeCode: "CORRECTIVE",
  status: { id: 1, name: "Open" },
  workflow: { id: "wf-abc", name: "Safety Workflow" },
  evidence: []
}

examples.name: Flow 1: Create an Action Example 4
examples.code:
SaveActionRequestValidator
  -> name required
  -> status EnumDTO id in range
  -> actionTypeCode present
SaveActionCommand
  -> validates actionTypeCode against active tenant ActionTypeDefinition rows
  -> rejects unknown or inactive codes with field-level errors

examples.name: Flow 1: Create an Action Example 5
examples.code:
SaveActionCommand
  -> creates ActionsDbContext through IDbContextFactory when no shared context is supplied
  -> validates catalogue references
  -> maps the aggregate root
  -> diffs child collections
  -> saves all changes in one transaction when required

examples.name: Flow 1: Create an Action Example 6
examples.code:
SaveActionEndpoint (continued)
  │ result.Succeeded → true
  │ var mapResult = result.Value!
  │ mapResult.NewItems.Count > 0 → true → CREATE branch
  │
  │ auditService.LogAsync("action.created", "Action", entityId,
  │   { Name: "Perform Safety Inspection" }, ct)
  │
  │ Send.CreatedAtAsync<GetActionEndpoint>(
  │   routeValues: { id = entityId },
  │   response: SaveActionResponse(entityId),
  │   ct)
  ▼
HTTP 201 Created
  │ Location: /api/v1/actions/019abc...
  │ Body: { "id": "019abc..." }

examples.name: Flow 1: Create an Action Example 7
examples.code:
ActionService.save() resolves → { id: '019abc...' }
  ▼
ActionCaptureComponent.onSave() (continued)
  │ isEditMode() was false → create flow
  │ toastr.success('Action created')
  │ router.navigate(['/actions', '019abc...'])
  │ saving.set(false)
  ▼
Router navigates to /actions/019abc...
  │ :id = '019abc...' → isEditMode = true
  │ effect() fires → loadEntity('019abc...')
  │ GET /api/v1/actions/019abc...
  │ Form populated with server state
  │ User is now in edit mode

examples.name: What happens when a user modifies an existing action.
examples.code:
ActionListComponent
  │ User double-clicks "Perform Safety Inspection" row
  │ onRowDoubleClicked(row) → router.navigate(['/actions', row.id])
  ▼
ActionCaptureComponent
  │ id input = '019abc...'
  │ isEditMode = true
  │ effect() fires → loadEntity('019abc...')

examples.name: Flow 2: Edit an Action Example 2
examples.code:
GET /api/v1/actions/{id}
  -> returns actionTypeCode and actionTypeName snapshot data
  -> returns status as EnumDTO
  -> returns workflow as NamedDTO
  -> component stores the aggregate in capture state

examples.name: Flow 2: Edit an Action Example 3
examples.code:
POST /api/v1/actions
{
  id: "019abc...",
  name: "Perform Monthly Safety Inspection",
  actionTypeCode: "CORRECTIVE",
  status: { id: 1, name: "Open" },
  workflow: { id: "wf-abc", name: "Safety Workflow" },
  evidence: []
}

Command validates actionTypeCode and then saves the aggregate.

examples.name: What happens when the list page loads its data.
examples.code:
POST /api/v1/actions/grid-list
  -> body carries Skip, Take, Sort, Filter, Search
  -> endpoint runs data and count queries in parallel
  -> DTOMapper returns flat rows with actionTypeCode/actionTypeName and statusName display fields
  -> nxgn server grid renders the returned projection

examples.name: Flow 4: Delete Actions Example 1
examples.code:
User selects 2 rows on grid → clicks Delete
  ▼
ActionListComponent.onDelete()
  │ selected = [{ id: 'aaa' }, { id: 'bbb' }]
  ▼
confirmService.confirmDelete(
  'Delete Actions',
  'Are you sure you want to delete 2 actions?')
  │ Modal dialog → user clicks "Delete"
  ▼
actionService.delete(['aaa', 'bbb'])
  │ POST /api/v1/actions/delete
  │ Body: { entityKeys: ['aaa', 'bbb'] }
  ▼
DeleteActionEndpoint
  │ var result = deleteCommand.ExecuteAsync(['aaa', 'bbb'], ct)
  │
  │ Command:
  │   Protection check → if protected, return CommandResult.Fail(...)
  │   BEGIN TRANSACTION
  │     DELETE FROM actions.evidence WHERE ActionId IN ('aaa','bbb')
  │     DELETE FROM actions.consultations WHERE ActionId IN ('aaa','bbb')
  │     DELETE FROM actions.actions WHERE Id IN ('aaa','bbb')
  │   COMMIT
  │   return CommandResult.Success
  │
  │ Returns CommandResult
  ▼
Endpoint (continued):
  │ if (!result.Succeeded) → SendProblemAsync(result.Error) → return
  │ auditService.LogAsync("action.deleted", "Action", 'aaa', {}, ct)
  │ auditService.LogAsync("action.deleted", "Action", 'bbb', {}, ct)
  │ Send.NoContentAsync(ct)
  ▼
HTTP 204 No Content
  ▼
ActionListComponent
  │ toastr.success('2 actions deleted')
  │ loadGrid() → refreshes data

examples.name: Flow 5: Aggregate with Children (Action with Evidence) Example 1
Flow 5: Aggregate with Children (Action with Evidence) Example 2
examples.code:
User clicks [+ Add Evidence] on embedded list
  │ Router → /actions/action-123/evidence/new
  │ Parent content hidden (d-none), child route activates
  ▼
EvidenceCaptureComponent
  │ Injects ActionCaptureState
  │ newRecordId = crypto.randomUUID() → 'temp-C'
  ▼
User fills form: description = 'Updated floor plan', fileUrl = '...'
  ▼
User clicks Save (child form)
  │ state.evidence.update(list => [...list, {
  │   id: 'temp-C',          ← temp client-side UUID
  │   description: 'Updated floor plan',
  │   fileUrl: '...', ...
  │ }])
  │ Router navigates back to /actions/action-123
  │ Parent content shown, embedded list reflects 3 evidence items

examples.name: Flow 5: Aggregate with Children (Action with Evidence) Example 3
examples.code:
User selects 'Compliance checklist scan' on embedded list → clicks Delete
  │ state.evidence.update(list => list.filter(e => e.id !== 'ev-B'))
  │ Embedded list now shows 2 items: [ev-A, temp-C]

examples.name: Flow 5: Aggregate with Children (Action with Evidence) Example 4
examples.code:
ActionCaptureComponent.onSave()
  -> existing child ids stay unchanged
  -> temporary child ids become Guid.Empty
  -> removed children are absent from the request
  -> command diffs existing vs incoming children
  -> command validates catalogue references before SaveChangesAsync

examples.name: Flow 5: Aggregate with Children (Action with Evidence) Example 5
examples.code:
Frontend                        Wire (JSON)                     Backend
────────                        ───────────                     ───────

Existing, unchanged:
  id: 'ev-A'                →   id: 'ev-A'                 →   Found by ID → update fields
  (server ID preserved)         (non-empty GUID)                result.UpdatedItems

New item:
  id: crypto.randomUUID()  →   id: '00000000-...'         →   Id == Guid.Empty → create
  (temp client-side UUID)       (mapped to EMPTY_GUID)          Guid.CreateVersion7()
                                                                result.NewItems

Removed item:
  (absent from collection) →   (not in request)           →   In existing, not in incoming
                                                                → delete (child diff)

examples.name: Data Shape at Each Layer Example 1
examples.code:
Tenant catalogue row      ActionTypeDefinition      Code = CORRECTIVE
EF/Postgres reference     VARCHAR or FK             CORRECTIVE or ActionTypeDefinitionId
DTOMapper                 Code/display snapshot     ActionTypeCode = CORRECTIVE
HTTP JSON                 string field              { "actionTypeCode": "CORRECTIVE" }
Command validation        active tenant lookup      rejects unknown or inactive codes
EntityMapper              copies accepted value     ActionTypeCode = dto.ActionTypeCode

sections.title: The Contract Boundary
content: The Angular frontend and .NET API backend communicate through a shared contract: the DTO shapes defined in `Nxgn.Actions.Contracts`. The frontend TypeScript interfaces mirror these C# classes exactly. This enables the **zero-transformation roundtrip** pattern:
│           Angular Web           │         │           .NET API              │
│  interface ActionDTO {          │  ←──→   │  class ActionDTO {              │
│    id: string;                  │  HTTP   │    Guid Id { get; set; }        │
│    name: string;                │  JSON   │    string Name { get; set; }    │
│    priority: EnumDTO;           │         │    EnumDTO Priority ...         │
│    status: EnumDTO;             │         │    EnumDTO Status ...           │
│    evidence: EvidenceDTO[];     │         │    List<EvidenceDTO> Evidence   │
│  // GET → DTO → mutate → POST  │         │  // SaveRequest inherits DTO    │
│  // Same shape in and out       │         │  // Guid.Empty = create         │
**Key rules at the boundary:**
- `EnumDTO { id: number, name: string }` crosses the wire — dropdown binding in Angular, `(Enum)dto.Field.Id` cast in C#
- `NamedDTO { id: string, name: string }` for FK references — Angular binds name directly, C# reads `.Id`
- `EMPTY_GUID` (`'00000000-...'` / `Guid.Empty`) signals "create this entity"
- Non-empty GUID signals "update this entity"
- Absent child items signal "delete these items" (detected via server-side diff)

sections.title: Flow 1: Create an Action
content: Create flow uses the client-side aggregate root pattern and catalogue lookup data loaded from the API.
```text
ActionCaptureComponent opens /actions/new
-> loads action type lookups from the owning admin module
-> user selects an active action type code such as CORRECTIVE
-> save request carries actionTypeCode, status EnumDTO, workflow NamedDTO, and child collections
-> SaveActionCommand validates actionTypeCode against active tenant catalogue records
-> EntityMapper copies the accepted code and maps true system enums
-> command saves root and children in one transaction
Action Type is tenant configuration, not a local enum. A clean-system browser E2E path must create or activate action types before this flow runs.

sections.title: Flow 2: Edit an Action
content: Edit flow reloads the aggregate root, preserves server identities for existing children, and posts the same roundtrip shape back to SAVE.
```text
GET /api/v1/actions/{id}
-> returns actionTypeCode and display snapshot data
-> component stores the full aggregate in capture state
-> user edits scalar fields or children
-> save request keeps existing child ids, maps temporary child ids to Guid.Empty, and carries actionTypeCode
-> command validates catalogue references and diffs child collections server-side
The frontend never translates enum aliases. The backend rejects unknown or inactive catalogue codes with field-level validation.

sections.title: Flow 3: Load the Grid
content: Grid load uses the server-grid endpoint and parallel count/data queries.
```text
POST /api/v1/actions/grid-list
-> request carries Skip, Take, Sort, Filter, Search
-> endpoint builds query parameters
-> data query and count query run in parallel
-> DTOMapper returns flat grid items with status display and actionTypeCode/actionTypeName snapshot fields
-> nxgn server grid renders rows without local lookup translation
Grid projections may flatten catalogue display values because they are read-only. Capture DTOs still use the roundtrip contract required by SAVE.

sections.title: Flow 4: Delete Actions
content: What happens when a user deletes selected actions. Delete returns `CommandResult` — endpoint checks `Succeeded`.
User selects 2 rows on grid → clicks Delete
ActionListComponent.onDelete()
│ selected = [{ id: 'aaa' }, { id: 'bbb' }]
confirmService.confirmDelete(
'Delete Actions',
'Are you sure you want to delete 2 actions?')
│ Modal dialog → user clicks "Delete"
actionService.delete(['aaa', 'bbb'])
│ POST /api/v1/actions/delete
│ Body: { entityKeys: ['aaa', 'bbb'] }
DeleteActionEndpoint
│ var result = deleteCommand.ExecuteAsync(['aaa', 'bbb'], ct)
│ Command:
│   Protection check → if protected, return CommandResult.Fail(...)
│   BEGIN TRANSACTION
│     DELETE FROM actions.evidence WHERE ActionId IN ('aaa','bbb')
│     DELETE FROM actions.consultations WHERE ActionId IN ('aaa','bbb')
│     DELETE FROM actions.actions WHERE Id IN ('aaa','bbb')
│   COMMIT
│   return CommandResult.Success
│ Returns CommandResult
Endpoint (continued):
│ if (!result.Succeeded) → SendProblemAsync(result.Error) → return
│ auditService.LogAsync("action.deleted", "Action", 'aaa', {}, ct)
│ auditService.LogAsync("action.deleted", "Action", 'bbb', {}, ct)
│ Send.NoContentAsync(ct)
HTTP 204 No Content
ActionListComponent
│ toastr.success('2 actions deleted')
│ loadGrid() → refreshes data

sections.title: Flow 5: Aggregate with Children (Action with Evidence)
content: Aggregate save with children keeps temporary client IDs local until SAVE.
```text
GET action aggregate
-> state.actionId = server id
-> state.evidence = existing child rows with server ids
Add child
-> child route creates a temporary browser UUID
-> parent state includes the new child for display
Save root
-> existing child ids stay unchanged
-> temporary child ids are sent as Guid.Empty
-> removed children are absent from the request
-> command diffs existing vs incoming children and saves INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE in the same DbContext transaction
Catalogue references on the root or child rows are validated by commands before persistence.

sections.title: Data Shape at Each Layer
content: ```text
Layer                    Shape                              Example Value
Tenant catalogue row      ActionTypeDefinition               Code = CORRECTIVE
EF/Postgres reference     VARCHAR or FK                      CORRECTIVE or ActionTypeDefinitionId
DTOMapper                 Code plus display snapshot         ActionTypeCode = CORRECTIVE, ActionTypeName = Corrective
C# DTO                    string code/display field          { ActionTypeCode: CORRECTIVE }
HTTP JSON                 string code/display field          { "actionTypeCode": "CORRECTIVE" }
TS interface              string code/display field          actionTypeCode: string
Angular signal            selected lookup DTO/code           { code: CORRECTIVE, name: Corrective }
Save request              same roundtrip contract            { actionTypeCode: CORRECTIVE }
Command validation         active tenant catalogue lookup     rejects unknown/inactive codes
EntityMapper              copies accepted value              ActionTypeCode = dto.ActionTypeCode
True system enums still use EnumDTO and enum casts. Tenant-configurable catalogues use owning admin module data and active-record validation.
